work_details.The Sickness Unto Death: An analysis of despair as the self's failure to be itself, and of faith as its only cure.

name: Karl Marx
central_ideas: Class struggle as motor of history
short_description: Put Hegel back on his feet: not Spirit but production and class conflict make history. The most consequential political philosopher of the modern age.

signature_quote.text: The philosophers have only interpreted the world, in various ways; the point is to change it.

idea_details.Material conditions shape consciousness: It is not consciousness that determines life but life — the material, economic conditions of production — that determines consciousness. Ideas reflect the social order that produces them.
idea_details.Class struggle as motor of history: History advances through conflict between classes defined by their relation to the means of production. The clash between exploiters and exploited drives social transformation.
idea_details.Alienated labor under capitalism: Under capitalism workers are estranged from their products, their activity, others, and their own human nature, since their labor is sold and controlled by another. Work becomes a loss of self.
idea_details.Critique of political economy: In Capital, Marx analyzes commodities, value, and surplus value to show that profit derives from unpaid labor — exposing exploitation beneath apparently free exchange.

work_details.Capital: Marx's monumental critique of political economy, analyzing the commodity, value, and surplus value to expose how capitalism extracts profit from labor.
work_details.The German Ideology: Co-written with Engels, it sets out the materialist conception of history: that material life, not ideas, shapes consciousness.
work_details.The Communist Manifesto: A short, fiery call to arms co-authored with Engels, framing history as class struggle and announcing the aims of communism.

name: Friedrich Engels
central_ideas: Origins of the family and the state
key_works: The Condition of the Working Class in England
The Origin of the Family, Private Property and the State
short_description: Without him there is no Marx as we have him. Funded, edited, and after Marx's death systematized the doctrine they had built together.

idea_details.Dialectics of nature: Engels sought to extend dialectical method beyond history into the natural world, reading processes in nature as dialectical.
idea_details.Origins of the family and the state: He gave a materialist account of how the family, private property, and the state arose together with class society.
idea_details.Collaborator and editor of Marx: Engels co-developed historical materialism with Marx, funded his work, and after his death edited the unfinished volumes of Capital.

work_details.The Condition of the Working Class in En: A pioneering empirical study of industrial poverty, based on Engels' firsthand observations in Manchester.
work_details.Anti-Dühring: A wide-ranging polemic that became a standard popular exposition of Marxist philosophy, economics, and socialism.
work_details.The Origin of the Family, Private Proper: A materialist history linking the rise of the family and the state to private property and class domination.

name: Friedrich Nietzsche
short_description: Suspicious of every inherited truth and moral value. Inverted the Platonic hierarchy: there is no truer world behind this one, only this world and its forces.

signature_quote.text: He who has a why to live for can bear almost any how.

idea_details.Death of God: Not a theological claim but a cultural diagnosis: the Christian-moral framework that grounded Western values has lost its credibility. Nietzsche asks whether we can face this loss without collapsing into nihilism.
idea_details.Will to power: Nietzsche's proposed basic drive of life — not mere survival but the striving to grow, overcome, and impose form. He reads psychology, morality, and culture as expressions of competing wills to power.
idea_details.Eternal return: A thought-experiment and a test: if you had to live this exact life over and over, infinitely, could you affirm it? To say yes is the mark of one who truly loves life rather than fleeing to a beyond.
idea_details.Genealogy of morals: Rather than asking whether moral values are true, Nietzsche traces their history — how 'good' and 'evil' arose, and whose interests they served. Master morality and the 'slave revolt' of ressentiment are his famous tools.
idea_details.The Übermensch: The 'overman' is the one who creates values out of his own affirmed life rather than inheriting them — a figure who appears once the old idols have fallen and humanity must give itself new goals.

work_details.Thus Spoke Zarathustra (1883): A philosophical prose-poem in which the prophet Zarathustra proclaims the death of God, the Übermensch, and the eternal return.
work_details.Beyond Good and Evil (1886): A wide-ranging critique of past philosophers and conventional morality, calling for a revaluation of values beyond good and evil.
work_details.On the Genealogy of Morality (1887): Three essays tracing the historical origins of moral concepts, including the 'slave revolt' of ressentiment and the meaning of ascetic ideals.

name: Jeremy Bentham
central_ideas: Greatest happiness of the greatest number
Critique of natural rights as 'nonsense on stilts'
key_works: An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
short_description: Tried to make ethics quantifiable. Pleasure plus, pain minus, sum across everyone affected: the right action is whichever maximizes the total.

idea_details.Greatest happiness of the greatest numbe: Bentham's principle of utility: the right action or policy is the one producing the greatest happiness for the greatest number of people.
idea_details.Hedonic calculus: He proposed measuring pleasures and pains by factors like intensity, duration, and certainty, to calculate which action yields the most net happiness.
idea_details.Critique of natural rights as 'nonsense: Bentham dismissed natural and imprescriptible rights as 'nonsense upon stilts,' recognizing only rights created by actual law.
idea_details.The Panopticon: He designed the Panopticon, a prison whose architecture lets a hidden inspector watch all inmates, embodying his utilitarian zeal for reform and oversight.

work_details.An Introduction to the Principles of Mor: Bentham's foundational statement of utilitarianism and the hedonic calculus as a basis for law and morality.

name: John Stuart Mill
short_description: Inherited Bentham's framework and humanized it: not all pleasures are equal, individual liberty matters, and women are not exceptions to either claim.

idea_details.Higher and lower pleasures: Mill refines utilitarianism by distinguishing qualities of pleasure: those engaging our higher faculties count for more. Better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied.
idea_details.Harm principle: The only legitimate ground for coercing an adult against their will is to prevent harm to others; one's own good is not sufficient warrant. A foundation of liberal liberty.
idea_details.Liberty of thought and discussion: Even a false opinion must be heard, for truth needs the challenge of error to remain living rather than dead dogma. Free discussion is essential to knowledge and progress.
idea_details.Subjection of women: Mill argues that the legal subordination of women is a relic of injustice that should give way to equality — both for justice's sake and for the good of society.

work_details.Utilitarianism: Mill's defense and refinement of the greatest-happiness principle, distinguishing higher from lower pleasures.
work_details.On Liberty: A classic argument for individual freedom, advancing the harm principle and the case for liberty of thought and discussion.
work_details.The Subjection of Women: An early and forceful argument that the legal subordination of women is unjust and should be replaced by equality.

name: Henry Sidgwick
short_description: Wrote what some still call the most careful book in ethics. Made utilitarianism survive serious self-examination, even when the conclusions worried him.

idea_details.The dualism of practical reason: Sidgwick identified an unresolved conflict between rational egoism and utilitarianism — reason seems to support both one's own good and the general good.
idea_details.Methodical comparison of ethical theorie: He carefully compared the main 'methods of ethics' — egoism, intuitionism, and utilitarianism — testing each for consistency.
idea_details.Common-sense morality: He examined the everyday moral rules of common sense, finding that they need a utilitarian principle to systematize and correct them.

work_details.The Methods of Ethics: His meticulous masterwork analyzing and comparing the major methods of ethical reasoning, admired for its rigour and honesty.

name: C. S. Peirce
key_works: How to Make Our Ideas Clear
short_description: Coined pragmatism and disowned it when James popularized it differently. Built the deepest logical and semiotic foundations of the American tradition.

idea_details.The pragmatic maxim: Peirce's rule for clarifying ideas: the meaning of a concept lies in the practical effects we conceive its object to have.
idea_details.Semiotic triadic sign: He developed a theory of signs in which meaning involves a triad of sign, object, and 'interpretant,' founding modern semiotics.
idea_details.Fallibilism: Peirce held that no belief is absolutely certain; all knowledge is provisional and open to revision by future inquiry.
idea_details.Abductive inference: He identified abduction — inference to the best explanation — as the creative step by which hypotheses are first proposed.

work_details.Collected Papers (posthumous): Peirce published little in book form; his vast, influential writings were gathered posthumously into the Collected Papers.
work_details.How to Make Our Ideas Clear: His seminal essay introducing the pragmatic maxim as a method for clarifying the meaning of concepts.

name: William James
short_description: Brought pragmatism to the public and married it to a generous psychology of belief. For James, ideas are tools and truth is what they earn us in experience.

idea_details.Truth as what works: For James, true beliefs are those that work — that guide us successfully through experience. Truth is not a static copy of reality but something that happens to an idea in practice.
idea_details.Stream of consciousness: Consciousness is not a chain of discrete states but a continuous, flowing stream, ever-changing and selective. James's image reshaped psychology and literature alike.
idea_details.The will to believe: Where evidence cannot decide and the choice is momentous, we may have a right to believe ahead of proof — as with moral or religious commitments that come true only if we trust them.
idea_details.Varieties of religious experience: James studies religion through lived personal experience rather than doctrine, treating its fruits in human life — not its metaphysics — as the measure of its value.

work_details.Pragmatism: James's popular lectures presenting truth as what works in experience and pragmatism as a method for settling disputes by their practical consequences.
work_details.The Principles of Psychology: A landmark work founding much of modern psychology, introducing ideas like the 'stream of consciousness.'
work_details.The Varieties of Religious Experience: A study of religion through firsthand personal experience rather than doctrine, judging faith by its fruits in life.

name: John Dewey
central_ideas: Democracy as a way of life
short_description: Extended pragmatism into education and politics. Saw democracy not as a procedure but as the social form in which inquiry, growth, and freedom converge.

idea_details.Inquiry as problem-solving: Dewey saw thinking as inquiry — a process triggered by a problematic situation and aimed at resolving it through experiment.
idea_details.Democracy as a way of life: For Dewey democracy is not just a form of government but a mode of associated living in which people share and grow together.
idea_details.Experimental ethics: He treated moral problems experimentally, as situations to be intelligently resolved rather than settled by fixed rules.
idea_details.Education through experience: He argued that genuine education arises from active, reflective experience, not the passive absorption of facts.

work_details.Democracy and Education: Dewey's major statement linking his philosophy of education to the ideals of democratic life.
work_details.Experience and Nature: A central work of his mature metaphysics, treating experience as continuous with nature.
work_details.The Quest for Certainty: A critique of philosophy's traditional search for absolute certainty, urging an experimental, fallibilist alternative.

name: Franz Brentano
central_ideas: Intentionality: every mental act is directed at an object
short_description: Rediscovered the medieval notion that the mind always intends something. The seed Husserl would grow into phenomenology.

idea_details.Intentionality: every mental act is dire: Brentano revived the idea that what marks the mental is intentionality — every thought, desire, or perception is about something.
idea_details.Descriptive vs. genetic psychology: He distinguished describing the structures of consciousness from explaining their causes, founding a descriptive psychology that fed phenomenology.
idea_details.Inner perception: He held that we have a special, evident inner perception of our own mental acts, distinct from outer observation.

work_details.Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint: His major work reintroducing intentionality and laying groundwork that Husserl would build into phenomenology.

name: Edmund Husserl
short_description: Tried to make philosophy a rigorous science of how things appear to consciousness. Bracketed the world to see it more clearly.

idea_details.Back to the things themselves: Phenomenology's rallying cry: set aside theories and describe experience exactly as it presents itself to consciousness, attending to the phenomena rather than imposing explanations.
idea_details.Phenomenological reduction (epoché): By 'bracketing' the question whether the world exists, Husserl suspends the natural attitude to focus on how things appear to consciousness, uncovering the structures of experience.
idea_details.Intentional consciousness: Consciousness is always consciousness of something — every mental act is directed toward an object. This 'aboutness,' or intentionality, is the basic structure phenomenology investigates.
idea_details.Lifeworld: The lifeworld is the pre-theoretical world of lived experience that underlies and is presupposed by science. Husserl's late work diagnoses the modern crisis as the forgetting of it.

work_details.Logical Investigations: The founding work of phenomenology, attacking psychologism and analyzing meaning and the intentional structure of consciousness.
work_details.Ideas I: Husserl's systematic introduction to pure phenomenology and the method of the reduction (epoché).
work_details.Cartesian Meditations: A late presentation of phenomenology modeled on Descartes, developing the transcendental ego and the problem of other minds.
work_details.Crisis of the European Sciences: Husserl's final work, diagnosing modern science's forgetting of the lived 'lifeworld' that gives it meaning.

name: Henri Bergson
short_description: Argued that life and time are lived from inside as flow and creation, not as measured intervals. A philosophy of duration against the clock.

idea_details.Duration (durée) vs. spatialized time: Bergson distinguished lived time — duration, a continuous flowing of qualitative change — from the measurable, spatialized time of science.
idea_details.The élan vital: He proposed an élan vital, a creative vital impulse driving evolution beyond mere mechanism toward novelty.
idea_details.Intuition as method: He held that intuition, not analysis, lets us grasp the inner flow of duration and life from within.
idea_details.Memory and matter: He explored how mind and body, memory and perception relate, arguing that memory is not stored in the brain like images in a box.

work_details.Time and Free Will: His first major work, distinguishing lived duration from spatial time and defending freedom.
work_details.Matter and Memory: An inquiry into the relation of mind and body through the problem of memory and perception.
work_details.Creative Evolution: His most famous work, introducing the élan vital and a vision of evolution as creative becoming.

name: Martin Heidegger
short_description: Reopened the question of the meaning of being, forgotten — he thought — since the Greeks. Read modernity from Descartes onward as the reduction of being to standing reserve.

signature_quote.text: Language is the house of being.

idea_details.The question of Being: Heidegger reopens the oldest question — what does it mean to be? — which he says philosophy has forgotten, taking 'being' for granted while studying only particular beings.
idea_details.Dasein (being-there): Heidegger calls the human being 'Dasein,' the entity for whom its own being is a question. We are the place where being becomes an issue, so the inquiry into being begins with us.
idea_details.Being-in-the-world: Dasein is not a subject set over against objects but always already involved in a world of meaningful, used things and relations. We understand ourselves through this practical immersion.
idea_details.Forgetting of Being: Western thought, from Plato on, has covered over the question of being, treating beings as objects merely present at hand. Heidegger seeks to retrieve what the tradition forgot.
idea_details.Essence of technology: Modern technology, for Heidegger, is a way of revealing that treats nature and people alike as 'standing reserve,' resources on call. Its danger is to crowd out other ways of disclosing the world.

work_details.Being and Time (1927): Heidegger's major work, reopening the question of Being through an analysis of human existence (Dasein) as care, temporality, and being-toward-death.
work_details.The Question Concerning Technology (1954: An essay arguing that modern technology is a way of revealing that reduces the world to 'standing reserve,' with both danger and a saving possibility.
work_details.Letter on Humanism (1947): A reply to Sartre distancing Heidegger from existentialism and locating the human essence in our relation to Being and language.

name: Karl Jaspers
key_works: The Origin and Goal of History
short_description: Brought existentialism into dialogue with psychiatry and history. For Jaspers, we are most ourselves at the limits — death, guilt, struggle, suffering.

idea_details.Limit situations (Grenzsituationen): Jaspers identified 'limit situations' — death, suffering, struggle, guilt — in which we confront the boundaries of existence and may awaken to authentic selfhood.
idea_details.Existenz and transcendence: He used 'Existenz' for the authentic self that relates to transcendence, the encompassing reality beyond all objects.
idea_details.Communication as condition of truth: He held that truth for existing individuals is realized through open, loving communication between persons.

work_details.Philosophy: His three-volume systematic work setting out his existentialist account of world-orientation, Existenz, and transcendence.
work_details.The Origin and Goal of History: A reflection on history introducing the idea of an 'Axial Age' that shaped humanity's spiritual foundations.
work_details.Reason and Existenz: Lectures clarifying the relation between reason and Existenz at the heart of his philosophy.

name: Jean-Paul Sartre
short_description: Made existentialism a public philosophy. We are condemned to be free; the meaning of who we are is the still-unfinished sum of what we choose.

signature_quote.text: Man is condemned to be free.

idea_details.Existence precedes essence: Humans first exist and then define themselves through their choices; there is no fixed human nature given in advance. We are nothing but what we make of ourselves.
idea_details.Radical freedom and bad faith: We are condemned to be free and wholly responsible for our choices. Bad faith is the self-deception by which we deny this freedom, pretending we are determined by role or circumstance.
idea_details.The look of the Other: Under another's gaze I become an object for them, which both threatens my freedom and reveals the reality of other minds. Sartre makes this 'look' central to human relations.
idea_details.Engaged literature: For Sartre the writer is responsible to their age; literature should commit itself, disclosing situations and calling readers to freedom rather than retreating into pure aesthetics.

work_details.Being and Nothingness: Sartre's massive treatise on phenomenological ontology, analyzing consciousness, radical freedom, bad faith, and the look of the Other.
work_details.Existentialism is a Humanism: A short, accessible lecture defending existentialism and summing up the slogan 'existence precedes essence.'
work_details.Critique of Dialectical Reason: Sartre's later attempt to reconcile existentialism with Marxism, analyzing how free individuals form groups and are shaped by history.

name: Simone de Beauvoir
central_ideas: One is not born, but becomes, a woman
short_description: Applied existentialism to the situation of women and showed that femininity is not a nature but a historical and social construction.

signature_quote.text: One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.

idea_details.One is not born, but becomes, a woman: Femininity is not a natural essence but something produced by history, upbringing, and society. 'Woman' is a situation one is shaped into, not a destiny one is born with.
idea_details.Woman as the Other: Man has been posited as the universal Subject and woman as the inessential 'Other,' defined only in relation to him. Beauvoir analyzes how this othering structures women's situation.
idea_details.Situated freedom: Freedom is always exercised within a concrete situation of body, history, and social constraint. Liberation requires changing those conditions, not merely asserting an abstract free will.
idea_details.Ethics of ambiguity: Human existence is ambiguous — we are both free subjects and objects in the world, both individual and bound to others. An authentic ethics embraces this and wills others' freedom along with one's own.

work_details.The Second Sex (1949): A landmark of feminist philosophy analyzing how woman has been constructed as the 'Other' — 'one is not born, but becomes, a woman.'
work_details.The Ethics of Ambiguity (1947): Beauvoir's existentialist ethics, embracing the ambiguity of the human condition and grounding morality in willing one's own and others' freedom.
work_details.The Coming of Age: A wide-ranging study of old age and how societies marginalize the elderly, companion to her analysis of woman's situation.

name: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
short_description: Brought the body back into phenomenology. We do not first think and then move; we inhabit a perceptual world through a body that already understands.

idea_details.Primacy of perception: Merleau-Ponty argued that perception is the basic mode of access to the world, prior to and grounding all reflection and science.
idea_details.The lived body: He placed the lived, experiencing body at the centre: we are not minds attached to objects but embodied subjects engaged with a world.
idea_details.Flesh of the world: In his late work he developed 'flesh' as the shared fabric in which perceiver and perceived, body and world, are intertwined.
idea_details.Embodied subjectivity: Against both empiricism and intellectualism, he showed subjectivity as bodily — perception and meaning arise through our being-in-the-world.

work_details.Phenomenology of Perception: His masterwork establishing the lived body and perception as the foundation of human existence.
work_details.The Visible and the Invisible: His unfinished late work developing the ontology of 'flesh,' the intertwining of body and world.

name: Albert Camus
short_description: Refused both nihilism and metaphysical consolation. The absurd is the silence between human demand for meaning and a world that doesn't answer.

idea_details.The absurd: The absurd arises from the clash between our craving for meaning and a silent, indifferent universe. It lies not in us alone nor the world alone but in their confrontation.
idea_details.Revolt without hope: Camus' response to the absurd is neither suicide nor false hope but revolt — living fully and lucidly in defiance of meaninglessness. One must imagine Sisyphus happy.
idea_details.Solidarity through rebellion: In The Rebel, the act of rebellion affirms a value shared with others — 'I rebel, therefore we are' — grounding human solidarity and setting limits against murder and tyranny.

work_details.The Myth of Sisyphus: Camus's philosophical essay on the absurd, asking whether life is worth living and answering with lucid revolt — 'one must imagine Sisyphus happy.'
work_details.The Stranger: A spare novel whose detached narrator, Meursault, embodies the confrontation with an indifferent universe.
work_details.The Rebel: An essay on rebellion through history, defending revolt that affirms shared human dignity while rejecting murderous revolution.

name: Emmanuel Levinas
central_ideas: The face of the Other as ethical command
short_description: Against Heidegger's ontology, made ethics first. Before knowing or being, we are already addressed by another whose face forbids us to reduce them to ourselves.

idea_details.The face of the Other as ethical command: Levinas held that encountering the face of another person commands me ethically — 'thou shalt not kill' — before any choice or theory.
idea_details.Ethics as first philosophy: He argued that ethics, not metaphysics or epistemology, is first philosophy: responsibility for the Other precedes and grounds everything else.
idea_details.Critique of totality: He criticized Western philosophy's drive to reduce everything to a single totality, defending instead the infinity and irreducibility of the Other.

work_details.Totality and Infinity: His major work opposing the violence of 'totality' with the ethical relation to the infinite Other.
work_details.Otherwise than Being: His later, more radical work rethinking subjectivity as substitution and responsibility for the Other.

name: Gottlob Frege
short_description: Almost unknown in his lifetime. Invented modern formal logic and the framework of meaning that the entire analytic tradition operates within.

idea_details.Logicism: arithmetic reducible to logic: Frege aimed to show that arithmetic is grounded in logic alone, deriving numbers and their laws from purely logical principles.
idea_details.Sense and reference: He distinguished a term's reference (the object it denotes) from its sense (the mode of presentation), explaining how identity statements can be informative.
idea_details.Function-argument analysis of propositio: Replacing subject-predicate grammar, he analyzed propositions as functions applied to arguments, founding modern logic.
idea_details.Anti-psychologism: He insisted that logic and meaning are objective, not psychological — thoughts are not private mental events but shareable contents.

work_details.Begriffsschrift: His 'concept-script,' the work that founded modern formal logic with quantifiers and a rigorous notation.
work_details.The Foundations of Arithmetic: A philosophical defense of logicism, analyzing the concept of number with great care.
work_details.On Sense and Reference: The classic essay introducing the distinction between sense and reference.

name: Bertrand Russell
short_description: Rebelled against British idealism, formalized logic with Whitehead, and made philosophy of language a central discipline. Wrote with a clarity rare in his trade.

idea_details.Logical atomism: Russell held that the world and language can be analyzed into logically simple 'atoms' — basic facts and their constituents.
idea_details.Theory of descriptions: His analysis of phrases like 'the present King of France' showed how to handle empty descriptions logically without positing nonexistent objects.
idea_details.Russell's paradox: He discovered a paradox about the set of all sets not members of themselves, which undermined Frege's system and reshaped the foundations of mathematics.
idea_details.Logicism (with Whitehead): With Whitehead he attempted to derive mathematics from logic in the monumental Principia Mathematica.

work_details.Principia Mathematica (with Whitehead): The monumental three-volume attempt to derive mathematics from logic.
work_details.The Problems of Philosophy: A lucid short introduction to central philosophical questions, including knowledge and the external world.
work_details.On Denoting: His landmark essay presenting the theory of descriptions.

name: Alfred North Whitehead
short_description: Began as Russell's co-author in logic and ended building a speculative metaphysics in which reality consists not of things but of events of becoming.

idea_details.Process metaphysics: Whitehead held that reality is fundamentally process — a becoming of events — rather than a collection of static substances.
idea_details.Actual occasions of experience: He made the basic units of reality 'actual occasions,' momentary events of experience that grasp and synthesize their world.
idea_details.Co-author of Principia Mathematica: With Russell he co-authored Principia Mathematica before turning from logic to speculative metaphysics.

work_details.Principia Mathematica (with Russell): The monumental work, co-authored with Russell, deriving mathematics from logic.
work_details.Process and Reality: His major metaphysical work setting out a philosophy of organism and process.
work_details.Science and the Modern World: An accessible account of the rise of modern science and the worldview it implies, with his critique of materialism.

name: G. E. Moore
short_description: Refuted idealism by holding up his hand. His insistence that ordinary certainties outweigh exotic arguments set the tone of British analytic philosophy.

idea_details.Defense of common sense: Moore defended the commonsense view of the world against skeptical and idealist arguments, trusting ordinary certainties over clever theories.
idea_details.The naturalistic fallacy: He charged that defining 'good' in terms of any natural property (like pleasure) commits a fallacy, since one can always ask whether that property is itself good.
idea_details.Direct realism: He defended the view that we directly perceive a mind-independent external world.
idea_details.Here is one hand: His proof of an external world: holding up his hands, he argued these are things we know more surely than any premise leading to skepticism.

work_details.Principia Ethica: His influential work introducing the naturalistic fallacy and treating good as simple and indefinable.
work_details.A Defence of Common Sense: An essay defending ordinary beliefs about the world as more certain than philosophical doubts.
work_details.Proof of an External World: The lecture in which he offers his 'here is one hand' proof against external-world skepticism.

name: Ludwig Wittgenstein
central_ideas: Limits of language as limits of the world
Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent
short_description: Changed the course of philosophy of language twice. His late work refutes his early work: the rare case of a philosopher who becomes his own deepest critic.

signature_quote.text: The limits of my language mean the limits of my world.

idea_details.Limits of language as limits of the worl: In the Tractatus, what can be said maps what can be the case; the bounds of meaningful language are the bounds of my world. What lies beyond cannot be said, only shown.
idea_details.Picture theory of meaning (early): A proposition has sense by picturing a possible state of affairs, sharing a logical form with what it represents. Language works like a model of reality.
idea_details.Language games (late): The later Wittgenstein sees language as many overlapping 'games' — practices like ordering, joking, promising — each with its own rules. There is no single essence of language.
idea_details.Meaning as use: Do not ask for the meaning, ask for the use: a word's meaning is its role in the practices of a form of life, not a hidden object it stands for.
idea_details.Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one mu: The Tractatus' closing line: about ethics, the mystical, and the sense of the world — what cannot be put into factual language — one must remain silent.

work_details.Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (1921): Wittgenstein's early work, presenting the picture theory of meaning and the limits of language in numbered propositions, ending in silence about what cannot be said.
work_details.Philosophical Investigations (1953, post: His later masterpiece, overturning the Tractatus with the ideas of language games, meaning as use, and philosophy as therapy for conceptual confusion.

name: Rudolf Carnap
key_works: The Logical Structure of the World
short_description: Leader of the Vienna Circle. Tried to dissolve metaphysics by showing it consisted of statements that could not, in principle, be verified.

idea_details.Logical construction of the world: Carnap tried to show how all concepts could be logically constructed from a basis in immediate experience.
idea_details.Verification principle: With the Vienna Circle he held that a statement is meaningful only if it can in principle be verified by experience — rendering much metaphysics meaningless.
idea_details.Linguistic frameworks: He argued that questions about what exists are relative to a chosen linguistic framework; we adopt frameworks for their usefulness, not their truth.
idea_details.Critique of metaphysics: He sought to expose traditional metaphysical disputes as pseudo-problems generated by the misuse of language.

work_details.The Logical Structure of the World: His ambitious attempt to construct all knowledge logically from elementary experiences.
work_details.The Logical Syntax of Language: A major work treating philosophy as the logical analysis of the syntax of scientific language.

name: W. V. O. Quine
short_description: Demolished the empiricist picture from inside. Once the line between analytic and synthetic blurs, philosophy and science become continuous.

idea_details.Rejection of the analytic-synthetic dist: Quine attacked the supposed line between truths of meaning and truths of fact, a cornerstone of empiricism, as untenable.
idea_details.Holism of confirmation: He held that our beliefs face experience not one by one but as a whole 'web,' so any belief can be kept if we adjust others.
idea_details.Indeterminacy of translation: He argued there is no single correct translation between languages — the facts underdetermine meaning.
idea_details.Ontological relativity: What objects a theory commits us to depends on how it is interpreted; ontology is relative to a background language.
idea_details.Naturalized epistemology: He proposed that epistemology become a branch of empirical science, studying how we actually form beliefs from sensory input.

work_details.Two Dogmas of Empiricism: His landmark essay rejecting the analytic-synthetic distinction and reductionism, reshaping analytic philosophy.
work_details.Word and Object: His major book developing the indeterminacy of translation and his naturalistic view of language and ontology.

name: Saul Kripke
central_ideas: Wittgenstein on rules and private language
key_works: Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Language
short_description: Resurrected metaphysics inside analytic philosophy. Names refer rigidly across possible worlds, and some necessities can only be discovered empirically.

idea_details.Rigid designators: Kripke argued that names are 'rigid designators' that refer to the same object in every possible world, unlike descriptions.
idea_details.Necessary a posteriori truths: He showed that some necessary truths (like 'water is H2O') can be known only through experience, breaking the assumed link between the necessary and the a priori.
idea_details.Causal theory of reference: He proposed that a name refers via a historical causal chain from an initial baptism, not via a description in the speaker's mind.
idea_details.Wittgenstein on rules and private langua: He reconstructed a skeptical paradox about rule-following from Wittgenstein, arguing that no fact determines what a rule means.

work_details.Naming and Necessity: His hugely influential lectures introducing rigid designators, the necessary a posteriori, and the causal theory of reference.
work_details.Wittgenstein on Rules and Private Langua: His reconstruction of Wittgenstein's rule-following paradox, debated under the name 'Kripkenstein.'

name: Donald Davidson
tradition: Analytic / Philosophy of mind and language
short_description: Built a unified account of mind, language, and action on the bet that we can only interpret others by assuming, for the most part, that they make sense.

idea_details.Truth-conditional semantics: Davidson proposed that to give the meaning of a language's sentences is to give a theory of their truth conditions, on the model of Tarski.
idea_details.Anomalous monism: He argued that mental events are physical events, yet there are no strict laws connecting the mental and physical — a subtle non-reductive physicalism.
idea_details.Principle of charity: Interpreting others requires assuming they are largely rational and their beliefs mostly true, or interpretation cannot get started.
idea_details.Radical interpretation: He analyzed meaning by asking what it would take to interpret a speaker from scratch, with no prior knowledge of their language.

work_details.Essays on Actions and Events: A collection developing his theory of action, causation, and anomalous monism.
work_details.Inquiries into Truth and Interpretation: Essays setting out his truth-conditional semantics and theory of radical interpretation.

name: Hilary Putnam
central_ideas: Semantic externalism (meanings aren't in the head)
Brain in a vat thought experiment
short_description: Changed his mind in public, often, and with reasons. Each position — functionalism, externalism, internal realism — left a permanent mark on the field.

idea_details.Semantic externalism (meanings aren't in: Putnam argued through the Twin Earth thought experiment that the meaning of words like 'water' depends partly on the external environment, not on inner states alone.
idea_details.Functionalism in philosophy of mind: He proposed that mental states are defined by their functional roles — their causal relations to inputs, outputs, and other states — not by their physical makeup.
idea_details.Internal realism: In his middle period he defended 'internal realism,' the view that truth and reference make sense only within a conceptual scheme, not from a God's-eye view.
idea_details.Brain in a vat thought experiment: He argued that, given semantic externalism, the hypothesis 'I am a brain in a vat' is self-refuting and cannot be coherently asserted.

work_details.The Meaning of 'Meaning': The essay presenting the Twin Earth argument and semantic externalism — 'meanings just ain't in the head.'
work_details.Reason, Truth and History: The book developing internal realism and the brain-in-a-vat argument.
work_details.Representation and Reality: A work in which Putnam criticizes the functionalism he had earlier championed.

name: Wilfrid Sellars
central_ideas: Space of reasons vs. space of causes
key_works: Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind
short_description: Attacked the empiricist idea that perception delivers raw data outside conceptual norms. Knowing is taking part in a normative game of giving reasons.

idea_details.Myth of the Given: Sellars attacked the idea that perception delivers self-justifying 'given' data lying outside the conceptual order of reasons.
idea_details.Space of reasons vs. space of causes: He distinguished describing a state from placing it in the 'space of reasons' — the normative game of justifying and challenging claims.
idea_details.Scientific image vs. manifest image: He contrasted the 'manifest image' (the world as it appears in everyday experience) with the 'scientific image,' and asked how to reconcile them.

work_details.Empiricism and the Philosophy of Mind: His landmark critique of the Myth of the Given and defense of knowledge as a normative achievement.
work_details.Science, Perception and Reality: A collection including his account of the manifest and scientific images of the world.

name: Walter Benjamin
central_ideas: Theses on the philosophy of history
key_works: The Work of Art in the Age of its Technological Reproducibility
Theses on the Philosophy of History
short_description: Read 19th-century Paris like a sacred text, mixed Marx with Jewish messianism, and killed himself fleeing the Gestapo. Left fragments that have shaped cultural theory ever since.

idea_details.Aura and mechanical reproduction: Benjamin argued that mechanically reproduced art (photography, film) loses the unique 'aura' of the original, transforming how art is experienced and politicized.
idea_details.Theses on the philosophy of history: In his late theses he attacked history-as-progress, picturing the 'angel of history' blown backward into the future amid accumulating wreckage.
idea_details.Dialectical image: He sought 'dialectical images' in which past and present flash together in a charged moment, illuminating history.
idea_details.Critique of progress: He criticized the ideology of inevitable progress, insisting that every document of civilization is also one of barbarism.

work_details.The Work of Art in the Age of its Techno: His famous essay on how reproduction strips art of its aura and changes its social and political role.
work_details.The Arcades Project: His vast, unfinished montage-study of nineteenth-century Paris as the dreamworld of modern capitalism.
work_details.Theses on the Philosophy of History: His final, fragmentary reflections attacking historicism and the notion of progress.

name: Max Horkheimer
central_ideas: Eclipse of reason into instrumental reason
short_description: Director of the Frankfurt Institute. Argued that the Enlightenment had not failed by accident but by following its own logic toward calculation and domination.

idea_details.Traditional vs. critical theory: Horkheimer distinguished 'traditional theory,' which describes society as given, from 'critical theory,' which aims to change the conditions it analyzes.
idea_details.Eclipse of reason into instrumental reas: He diagnosed modern reason as shrinking into mere instrumental calculation of means, losing the capacity to reflect on ends.
idea_details.Dialectic of Enlightenment (with Adorno): With Adorno he argued that Enlightenment reason, by seeking to dominate nature, turned into a new mythology and domination.

work_details.Eclipse of Reason: His critique of how reason has degenerated into instrumental rationality in modern society.
work_details.Dialectic of Enlightenment (with Adorno): The foundational Frankfurt School work arguing that Enlightenment reason reverts to domination and myth.

name: Theodor Adorno
short_description: Wrote against systems while remaining a relentless thinker of totality. For him, the modern world's smoothness is itself the symptom that calls for relentless suspicion.

idea_details.Negative dialectics: Adorno's method keeps dialectical movement without Hegel's reconciling synthesis, insisting on what concepts fail to capture.
idea_details.Critique of the culture industry: He analyzed mass culture as an 'industry' that standardizes taste and pacifies, integrating people into the existing order.
idea_details.Identity-thinking and the non-identical: He criticized 'identity-thinking' that forces particulars under general concepts, defending the 'non-identical' that resists such reduction.
idea_details.After Auschwitz: He held that Auschwitz shatters culture's self-justification, placing new demands on thought, art, and morality.

work_details.Dialectic of Enlightenment: Co-written with Horkheimer, arguing that Enlightenment reason turns into domination and myth.
work_details.Negative Dialectics: His major philosophical work developing a dialectics that refuses final synthesis.
work_details.Minima Moralia: A book of aphoristic 'reflections from damaged life,' diagnosing modern existence under capitalism.

name: Herbert Marcuse
short_description: Brought the Frankfurt School to American radicalism in the 1960s. Diagnosed advanced capitalism as a regime that absorbs every protest into its own machinery.

idea_details.One-dimensional society: Marcuse argued that advanced industrial society flattens thought and dissent into one dimension, absorbing opposition into the system.
idea_details.Repressive desublimation: He coined this term for how consumer society grants a managed, superficial sexual freedom that defuses deeper rebellion.
idea_details.Great refusal: He looked to a 'Great Refusal' — radical rejection of the existing order — by those still outside its integrating logic.
idea_details.Liberation through aesthetics: He saw in art and the aesthetic dimension a utopian promise of liberated sensibility beyond domination.

work_details.One-Dimensional Man: His influential critique of advanced industrial society as smoothly suppressing genuine alternatives.
work_details.Eros and Civilization: A fusion of Freud and Marx arguing for a non-repressive civilization built on liberated Eros.

name: Erich Fromm
short_description: Asked why people, given freedom, so often rush back to authority. Married psychoanalysis to social critique with unusual public reach.

idea_details.Escape from freedom: Fromm analyzed how the burdens of modern freedom can drive people to surrender it to authority, conformity, or destructiveness.
idea_details.Authoritarian character: He described a character structure drawn to both submission and domination, helping explain the appeal of authoritarian movements.
idea_details.Having vs. being: He contrasted two modes of existence: the 'having' mode of possession and the 'being' mode of active, loving engagement.
idea_details.Art of loving: He treated love not as a feeling that befalls us but as an art and practice requiring knowledge, effort, and care.

work_details.Escape from Freedom: His analysis of why people flee the responsibilities of freedom into authority and conformity.
work_details.The Art of Loving: A widely read work arguing that love is a skill to be developed, not merely a feeling.
work_details.To Have or to Be?: A late work contrasting the modes of having and being as ways of life.

name: Jürgen Habermas
short_description: Pulled critical theory back from the despair of Adorno. The rationality the Enlightenment promised, he argued, lives not in calculation but in unforced agreement among speakers.

idea_details.Communicative action: Beyond strategic action aimed at success, Habermas identifies communicative action aimed at mutual understanding, where people coordinate through reasons rather than force.
idea_details.Ideal speech situation: A regulative ideal of discourse free of coercion and distortion, where only the force of the better argument prevails. It supplies a standard for when agreement is genuinely rational.
idea_details.Public sphere: The domain where private citizens come together to debate matters of common concern and form public opinion. Habermas traces both its rise and its erosion by commercial mass media.
idea_details.Procedural deliberative democracy: Legitimacy comes not from a fixed blueprint but from fair procedures of inclusive deliberation, in which norms are justified to all those affected.

work_details.The Theory of Communicative Action: Habermas's major work distinguishing strategic from communicative action and grounding social theory in the rationality of reaching understanding.
work_details.Knowledge and Human Interests: An analysis of how different forms of inquiry are guided by underlying human interests, situating critical theory among them.
work_details.Between Facts and Norms: Habermas's mature legal and political philosophy, developing a discourse theory of law and deliberative democracy.

name: Wilhelm Dilthey
central_ideas: Lived experience as ground of the human sciences
key_works: The Construction of the Historical World
short_description: Drew the line between the natural and the human sciences. Where physics explains, history understands — and understanding is its own discipline.

idea_details.Verstehen (understanding) vs. erklären (: Dilthey distinguished the human sciences, which 'understand' meaning from within, from the natural sciences, which 'explain' by causal law.
idea_details.Lived experience as ground of the human: He grounded the human sciences in lived experience (Erlebnis), the meaningful texture of historical and personal life.
idea_details.Hermeneutic circle: He emphasized that understanding moves in a circle between parts and whole, never from a presuppositionless start.

work_details.Introduction to the Human Sciences: His attempt to give the human sciences their own epistemological foundation distinct from natural science.
work_details.The Construction of the Historical World: A later work developing his hermeneutics and theory of historical understanding.

name: Hans-Georg Gadamer
short_description: Rehabilitated prejudice. Understanding never starts from zero; it is a dialogue between our horizon and the text's, in which neither stays the same.

idea_details.Fusion of horizons: Gadamer described understanding as a 'fusion of horizons,' in which the interpreter's world and the text's world meet and enlarge each other.
idea_details.Prejudice as condition of understanding: He rehabilitated 'prejudice' (pre-judgment) as the inescapable, enabling background from which all understanding begins.
idea_details.Tradition and dialogue: He stressed that we always understand from within a living tradition, engaging the past as a partner in dialogue.
idea_details.Play, festival, art: He used play, festival, and the artwork to show how meaning is an event we participate in rather than an object we master.

work_details.Truth and Method: His major work founding philosophical hermeneutics and arguing that understanding is more than method.

name: Paul Ricoeur
central_ideas: Hermeneutics of suspicion vs. of trust
short_description: Mediated between phenomenology, psychoanalysis, and analytic philosophy. We come to know ourselves not directly but through the long detour of the works we read.

idea_details.Hermeneutics of suspicion vs. of trust: Ricoeur contrasted interpretation that unmasks hidden motives (Marx, Nietzsche, Freud) with interpretation that recovers and restores meaning.
idea_details.Narrative identity: He argued that we make sense of ourselves through narrative — the self is the story it tells and is told about it.
idea_details.Symbol gives rise to thought: He held that rich symbols and texts provoke and nourish reflection: 'the symbol gives rise to thought.'

work_details.Freud and Philosophy: His major study of psychoanalysis, introducing the 'hermeneutics of suspicion.'
work_details.Time and Narrative: A three-volume work on how narrative shapes our experience of time and identity.
work_details.Oneself as Another: His mature work on selfhood, ethics, and narrative identity.

name: Ferdinand de Saussure
central_ideas: Language as a system of differences
short_description: Made linguistics a structural science. His students published lecture notes after his death that became the source of every structuralism that followed.

idea_details.Sign as signifier/signified: Saussure analyzed the linguistic sign as a union of a 'signifier' (sound-image) and a 'signified' (concept).
idea_details.Arbitrariness of the sign: He stressed that the link between signifier and signified is arbitrary — no natural reason a given sound means a given concept.
idea_details.Language as a system of differences: He argued that signs have meaning only through their differences from other signs in the system, not by any positive content.
idea_details.Synchronic vs. diachronic: He distinguished studying language as a system at a moment (synchronic) from studying its historical change (diachronic).

work_details.Course in General Linguistics (posthumou: Compiled by students from his lectures after his death, this work founded structural linguistics and shaped all of structuralism.

name: Claude Lévi-Strauss
short_description: Brought Saussurean structuralism into anthropology. Myths around the world, he argued, share a common combinatorial logic — and so do we.

idea_details.Structural analysis of myth: Lévi-Strauss analyzed myths as systems of binary oppositions, seeking the underlying structures common to human thought.
idea_details.Elementary structures of kinship: He treated kinship and marriage rules as structured systems of exchange, applying linguistic models to social life.
idea_details.The savage mind: He argued that so-called 'primitive' thought is as rigorous and logical as modern science, only differently oriented.
idea_details.Binary oppositions: He found binary oppositions (raw/cooked, nature/culture) to be a basic organizing principle of the human mind.

work_details.Tristes Tropiques: A reflective travel memoir and meditation on anthropology, civilization, and loss.
work_details.The Savage Mind: His argument that 'primitive' thought is a rigorous science of the concrete.
work_details.Structural Anthropology: Essays applying structural-linguistic methods to myth, kinship, and culture.

name: Jacques Lacan
central_ideas: The unconscious is structured like a language
short_description: Re-read Freud through Saussure and Hegel. The ego is a misrecognition; the unconscious is the discourse of the Other; meaning chains and slips.

idea_details.The unconscious is structured like a lan: Lacan reread Freud through structural linguistics, holding that the unconscious operates through chains of signifiers like a language.
idea_details.Mirror stage: He described how an infant, seeing its reflection, forms an idealized but illusory sense of a unified self — the basis of the ego.
idea_details.Imaginary / Symbolic / Real: He distinguished three registers: the Imaginary of images and identification, the Symbolic of language and law, and the Real that resists symbolization.
idea_details.Return to Freud: He called for a 'return to Freud,' recovering the radical core of psychoanalysis against its softened, adaptive versions.

work_details.Écrits: His dense, influential collected writings on psychoanalysis and language.
work_details.Seminars: The decades of seminars in which Lacan developed and revised his teaching before live audiences.

name: Michel Foucault
short_description: Studied how the modern subject is made by the institutions — prisons, clinics, confessionals — that claim only to know it. The knowledge is the power.

idea_details.Knowledge as power: Knowledge and power are intertwined: forms of knowledge both produce and are produced by relations of power. 'Power/knowledge' shapes what counts as truth and who may speak it.
idea_details.Discipline and biopolitics: Modern power works less by spectacular punishment than by discipline — surveillance, normalization, the training of bodies — and by a 'biopolitics' that manages populations and life itself.
idea_details.Archaeology and genealogy: Foucault's methods: archaeology excavates the hidden rules that make a discourse possible; genealogy traces how our practices and concepts emerged from contingent histories of struggle.
idea_details.The death of man: 'Man' as the central object of the human sciences is, Foucault argues, a recent invention that may pass away — like a face drawn in sand at the edge of the sea.
idea_details.Care of the self: In his late work Foucault turns to ancient ethics, exploring practices by which individuals shape themselves — an art of living and self-formation rather than mere obedience to a code.

work_details.Discipline and Punish: A history of the prison tracing the shift from public torture to disciplinary surveillance, and the making of the modern, normalized subject.
work_details.The History of Sexuality: A multi-volume study arguing that sexuality is not repressed but produced by discourses and power, turning later to ancient ethics of the self.
work_details.The Order of Things: An 'archaeology' of the human sciences, uncovering the shifting underlying codes of knowledge and announcing the recent 'death of man.'

name: Jacques Derrida
central_ideas: Critique of the metaphysics of presence
There is nothing outside the text
short_description: Showed how every text undermines its own claim to a stable center. Read Western philosophy as a long, repressed quarrel between speech and writing.

idea_details.Deconstruction: A way of reading that shows how texts undermine their own claims to a single, stable meaning, exposing the tensions and exclusions on which they depend. Not destruction but careful unsettling.
idea_details.Différance: Derrida's coinage: meaning arises through difference and is endlessly deferred, never fully present. The play of differences means no sign ever comes to rest in a final signified.
idea_details.Critique of the metaphysics of presence: Western thought, Derrida argues, privileges presence — the immediately given, speech over writing, origin over trace. Deconstruction questions this deep-seated preference.
idea_details.There is nothing outside the text: Often misread: the claim is that there is no meaning outside a web of contexts and traces; we never reach a pure, context-free reality behind all interpretation.

work_details.Of Grammatology: Derrida's foundational work introducing deconstruction and différance, and critiquing the privileging of speech over writing in Western thought.
work_details.Writing and Difference: A collection of essays applying deconstructive reading to figures from Hegel to Levinas and Freud.
work_details.Speech and Phenomena: A close deconstructive reading of Husserl, arguing that phenomenology cannot secure the pure self-presence it seeks.

name: Gilles Deleuze
central_ideas: Difference in itself, not as opposition
short_description: Reread Spinoza, Nietzsche, and Bergson into a philosophy of pure difference and becoming. Wrote with Guattari a manifesto against psychoanalysis and the State-form of thought.

idea_details.Difference in itself, not as opposition: Deleuze sought to think difference positively, in itself, rather than as the negation or opposite of identity.
idea_details.Rhizome vs. tree: With Guattari he contrasted the branching 'tree' of hierarchical thought with the 'rhizome,' a non-hierarchical network with no centre.
idea_details.Immanence: He championed a philosophy of pure immanence, with no transcendent beyond, in the lineage of Spinoza.
idea_details.Bodies without organs: He used the 'body without organs' to figure a body freed from fixed organization — a field of intensities and becomings.
idea_details.Affirmation against negation: Following Nietzsche, he prized affirmation and creation over the negative, reactive forces of opposition and resentment.

work_details.Difference and Repetition: His major independent work developing a metaphysics of difference and repetition.
work_details.A Thousand Plateaus (with Guattari): A sprawling, experimental work introducing the rhizome and many of his and Guattari's key concepts.
work_details.Anti-Oedipus (with Guattari): Their polemical fusion of Marx and Freud attacking psychoanalysis and capitalism's organization of desire.

name: Jean-François Lyotard
short_description: Defined the postmodern as the loss of faith in the big stories — progress, emancipation, totality — that modernity told to justify itself.

idea_details.Incredulity toward metanarratives: Lyotard defined the postmodern as 'incredulity toward metanarratives' — loss of faith in the grand stories (progress, emancipation) that legitimized modernity.
idea_details.The differend: He analyzed the 'differend,' a conflict between parties that cannot be justly resolved because they lack a common rule of judgment.
idea_details.Sublime and the postmodern: He linked the postmodern to the Kantian sublime — the attempt to present the unpresentable, gesturing beyond what representation can capture.

work_details.The Postmodern Condition: His influential report defining the postmodern as incredulity toward metanarratives.
work_details.The Differend: His major philosophical work on conflicts that admit no common rule of judgment.

name: Jean Baudrillard
short_description: Argued that signs have detached from anything they once represented. We live in a hyperreal world of copies without originals — and have stopped noticing.

idea_details.Simulacra and simulation: Baudrillard argued that signs and copies ('simulacra') have detached from any reality, so that simulation precedes and replaces the real.
idea_details.Hyperreality: He described a 'hyperreal' condition in which models and media are more real to us than reality itself.
idea_details.The end of the real: He claimed that in media-saturated society the distinction between the real and its representation has collapsed.
idea_details.Symbolic exchange: He contrasted modern sign-value with archaic 'symbolic exchange,' a reciprocal logic he saw as lost to consumer society.

work_details.Simulacra and Simulation: His best-known work on simulation, hyperreality, and the disappearance of the real.
work_details.The System of Objects: An early work analyzing consumer society through the meanings of everyday objects.

name: Hannah Arendt
central_ideas: The human condition: labor, work, action
Plurality as the basic political fact
short_description: A student of Heidegger and Jaspers who reinvented political philosophy after Auschwitz. Showed that ordinary thoughtlessness, not satanic depth, makes most evil possible.

idea_details.The human condition: labor, work, action: Arendt distinguishes labor (meeting biological needs), work (making durable things), and action (speech and deed among others). Action, which discloses who we are, is the properly political activity.
idea_details.Banality of evil: Reporting on Eichmann, Arendt argues that great evil can stem not from monstrous motives but from thoughtlessness — the failure to think and judge — in an ordinary functionary.
idea_details.Plurality as the basic political fact: Politics arises because not Man but men, in their plurality, inhabit the earth. We are each distinct, and this plurality is the condition of action and of a shared public world.
idea_details.Public realm and natality: Freedom appears in a public realm where people act and speak together. 'Natality' — the human capacity to begin something new, rooted in birth — is the wellspring of action and hope.

work_details.The Origins of Totalitarianism: Arendt's study of antisemitism, imperialism, and totalitarian movements, analyzing how Nazi and Stalinist regimes destroyed the capacity for action.
work_details.The Human Condition: Her central work distinguishing labor, work, and action, and lamenting the modern eclipse of genuine political action.
work_details.Eichmann in Jerusalem: Her controversial report on the trial of Adolf Eichmann, introducing the phrase 'the banality of evil.'

name: John Rawls
short_description: Almost single-handedly revived Anglophone political philosophy. Asked: which principles of justice would we choose if we did not know where in society we would end up?

idea_details.Justice as fairness: Rawls conceives the principles of justice as those that free and equal persons would agree to under fair conditions. Justice is what fair procedures of choice would yield.
idea_details.Veil of ignorance: To ensure fairness, we imagine choosing principles without knowing our place in society — our class, talents, or beliefs. Ignorance of our own position blocks self-serving bias.
idea_details.Two principles of justice: Behind the veil we would choose equal basic liberties for all, and allow social and economic inequalities only if they benefit the least advantaged and attach to positions open to all.
idea_details.Overlapping consensus: A just, stable society can be endorsed from within many different moral and religious worldviews, which converge on shared political principles for their own distinct reasons.

work_details.A Theory of Justice: Rawls's landmark revival of political philosophy, deriving principles of justice from a fair 'original position' behind a veil of ignorance.
work_details.Political Liberalism: Rawls's reworking of his theory to show how a just society can be stable amid deep disagreement, through an overlapping consensus.

name: Robert Nozick
short_description: Replied to Rawls from the right: any state larger than a minimal night-watchman is a violation of individual rights, however well-intentioned its redistributions.

idea_details.Entitlement theory of justice: Nozick held that a distribution is just if it arises from just acquisition and voluntary transfer, regardless of the pattern it produces.
idea_details.Minimal state: He argued that only a minimal 'night-watchman' state, limited to protection against force and fraud, is morally justified.
idea_details.Wilt Chamberlain argument: He used the example of fans freely paying to watch Wilt Chamberlain to show that maintaining any fixed distribution requires constant interference with liberty.
idea_details.Experience machine: His thought experiment of a machine giving any desired experiences argues that we value more than pleasure — we want to actually do things and be a certain way.

work_details.Anarchy, State, and Utopia: His libertarian classic defending the minimal state and the entitlement theory of justice, written partly in reply to Rawls.
work_details.Philosophical Explanations: A wide-ranging later work on knowledge, free will, identity, and the meaning of life.

name: Alasdair MacIntyre
short_description: Argued that modern moral discourse is wreckage: fragments of a virtue tradition torn from the practices and communities that made it intelligible.

idea_details.Tradition-constituted rationality: MacIntyre argued that standards of reasoning are not neutral but develop within historical traditions of inquiry.
idea_details.Practices and internal goods: He analyzed 'practices' with their internal goods and standards of excellence as the setting in which virtues are exercised.
idea_details.The fragmentation of moral language: He claimed modern moral discourse is a wreckage of fragments torn from the traditions that once made them coherent, leaving interminable disagreement.

work_details.After Virtue: His influential diagnosis of modern moral disorder and call to recover an Aristotelian ethics of the virtues.
work_details.Whose Justice? Which Rationality?: Its sequel, arguing that justice and rationality are intelligible only within particular traditions.

name: Charles Taylor
central_ideas: Multiple modernities and the secular age
short_description: Brought Hegel and Heidegger to bear on liberal political theory. Mapped how modern selves came to feel like they sit inside their own skulls.

idea_details.Strong evaluation and selfhood: Taylor argued that selfhood involves 'strong evaluation' — qualitative distinctions of worth that frame our identity and orient our lives.
idea_details.Multiple modernities and the secular age: He traced how the modern secular condition arose, making unbelief one option among many rather than a simple subtraction of religion.
idea_details.Politics of recognition: He analyzed the modern demand that our identities be recognized, a key to debates over multiculturalism.

work_details.Sources of the Self: His sweeping history of how the modern identity, with its inwardness and affirmation of ordinary life, came to be.
work_details.A Secular Age: His major account of how Western society moved from a world where belief was axiomatic to one where it is contested.

name: Judith Butler
short_description: Argued that gender is not what you are but what you do — and that the doing is enforced by norms that punish deviation. Recast feminist theory and queer studies.

idea_details.Gender performativity: Butler argued that gender is not an inner essence but is produced through repeated performances — stylized acts that create the illusion of a stable gender.
idea_details.Critique of the subject: Drawing on Foucault, Butler questioned the idea of a pre-given subject behind action, seeing the subject as an effect of power and norms.
idea_details.Precarious life and grievability: In later work Butler explored how social norms make some lives count as grievable and others not, grounding an ethics of shared vulnerability.

work_details.Gender Trouble: The hugely influential work introducing gender performativity and reshaping feminist and queer theory.
work_details.Bodies That Matter: A follow-up clarifying performativity and addressing the materiality of the body.
work_details.Precarious Life: Essays on vulnerability, mourning, and ethics written after 9/11.

name: Luce Irigaray
central_ideas: This sex which is not one
Sexual difference as the philosophical question of our age
key_works: This Sex Which Is Not One
short_description: Read the history of philosophy as written in a single voice that called itself neutral and was male. Demanded a language in which two sexes can speak.

idea_details.This sex which is not one: Irigaray argued that female sexuality and subjectivity have been defined only in masculine terms, calling for a femininity articulated on its own.
idea_details.Sexual difference as the philosophical q: She held that thinking genuine sexual difference is the central philosophical task of our time.
idea_details.Critique of phallogocentrism: She criticized Western thought and language as 'phallogocentric' — organized around a single masculine standard that erases the feminine.

work_details.Speculum of the Other Woman: Her landmark work re-reading the philosophical and psychoanalytic canon to expose its masculine bias.
work_details.This Sex Which Is Not One: Essays developing her thought on female subjectivity, language, and sexual difference.

name: Julia Kristeva
short_description: Brought psychoanalysis, linguistics, and literary theory together. Located meaning not only in the symbolic order but in the bodily rhythms that disrupt it.

idea_details.The semiotic and the symbolic: Kristeva distinguished the 'symbolic' order of language and law from the 'semiotic,' the bodily rhythms and drives that disrupt and energize it.
idea_details.Abjection: She analyzed 'abjection,' our reaction of horror at what threatens the boundaries of the self, central to identity and culture.
idea_details.Intertextuality: She coined 'intertextuality' for how every text is woven from other texts, with no self-contained meaning.
idea_details.Strangers to ourselves: She explored foreignness, arguing that we are 'strangers to ourselves' — confronting the other within is the basis for living with others.

work_details.Revolution in Poetic Language: Her major theoretical work introducing the semiotic and the symbolic and analyzing avant-garde poetry.
work_details.Powers of Horror: Her influential study of abjection in literature and culture.
work_details.Strangers to Ourselves: A reflection on foreignness drawing on psychoanalysis and the history of the foreigner.

name: Karl Popper
key_works: The Open Society and Its Enemies
short_description: Replaced verification with falsification as the mark of science. Also wrote one of the great defenses of liberal democracy against Plato, Hegel, and Marx.

idea_details.Falsifiability as criterion of science: What makes a theory scientific is not that it can be proved but that it can be refuted: a genuine scientific claim forbids something and so risks being shown false.
idea_details.Open society: Popper defends the open society — pluralist, self-critical, reformable piecemeal — against 'closed' societies governed by a fixed blueprint, which he traces to Plato, Hegel, and Marx.
idea_details.Critique of historicism: He attacks 'historicism,' the belief that history obeys discoverable laws enabling large-scale prediction. Such claims, he argues, are unscientific and underwrite totalitarian politics.
idea_details.Conjectures and refutations: Knowledge grows by bold conjecture and severe testing: we propose theories and try hard to refute them, learning from our errors. There is no certain foundation, only better-tested guesses.

work_details.The Logic of Scientific Discovery: Popper's foundational work proposing falsifiability as the criterion of science and rejecting induction in favor of conjecture and refutation.
work_details.The Open Society and Its Enemies: A passionate defense of liberal democracy and critique of the authoritarian strands in Plato, Hegel, and Marx.
work_details.Conjectures and Refutations: Essays developing his view that knowledge grows through bold hypotheses subjected to severe testing.

name: Thomas Kuhn
short_description: Argued that science does not advance by steady accumulation but by ruptures in which entire ways of seeing the world get replaced.

idea_details.Paradigm shifts: Kuhn argued that science does not progress by steady accumulation but through revolutionary 'paradigm shifts' that replace one framework with another.
idea_details.Normal vs. revolutionary science: He distinguished 'normal science,' puzzle-solving within a paradigm, from 'revolutionary science,' when anomalies force a paradigm's collapse.
idea_details.Incommensurability: He claimed rival paradigms can be 'incommensurable' — their concepts and standards so different that they cannot be straightforwardly compared.
idea_details.Disciplinary matrices: He refined 'paradigm' into the shared commitments, models, and exemplars that bind a scientific community.

work_details.The Structure of Scientific Revolutions: His enormously influential book introducing paradigms, normal science, and scientific revolutions.

name: Imre Lakatos
key_works: The Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes
short_description: Tried to save Popperian rationality from Kuhnian crisis. Science is neither a single test nor a paradigm flip, but competing programmes evolving over time.

idea_details.Research programmes: Lakatos analyzed science in terms of 'research programmes' that evolve over time, rather than single theories tested in isolation.
idea_details.Hard core and protective belt: Each programme has a 'hard core' of central assumptions shielded by a 'protective belt' of auxiliary hypotheses that absorb refutations.
idea_details.Rational reconstruction of science: He sought to reconstruct scientific progress rationally, distinguishing 'progressive' programmes that predict novel facts from 'degenerating' ones.

work_details.The Methodology of Scientific Research P: His central work reconciling Popper and Kuhn through the idea of competing research programmes.

name: Paul Feyerabend
short_description: The bad boy of philosophy of science. Argued that no method explains the actual triumphs of science, and that monopolies of truth — scientific or otherwise — are dangerous.

idea_details.Against method: Feyerabend argued that no fixed method explains the actual successes of science; rigid rules would have stifled its greatest advances.
idea_details.Anything goes: He provocatively concluded that the only principle that does not inhibit progress is 'anything goes.'
idea_details.Theoretical pluralism: He defended a proliferation of competing theories as essential to testing and progress.
idea_details.Critique of scientific authority: He challenged science's privileged authority, urging that it not be imposed dogmatically over other traditions in a free society.

work_details.Against Method: His iconoclastic attack on methodological rules in science, arguing for epistemological anarchism.
work_details.Science in a Free Society: A defense and extension of his views, questioning the dominance of science over other forms of knowledge.
